data.title: 4 Things Investors Are Watching as Markets Reopen After the Long Weekend
data.description:
U.S. markets are reopening after a holiday, as investors return to trading following developments that emerged while markets were closed. With no trading on Monday due to the Martin Luther King Jr. holiday, markets now need to catch up on trade headlines, early earnings signals, and interest-rate expectations. Here are the key issues investors are watching as trading resumes.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

1. Trade headlines finally hit markets: Over the weekend, President Donald Trump said the U.S. would impose new tariffs linked to Greenland. He announced a 10% tariff on imports from eight European countries starting February 1, with the rate set to rise to 25% by June if no deal is reached to acquire Greenland. The comments came while U.S. markets were closed, delaying any immediate reaction. As trading resumes, investors are watching whether tariff concerns weigh on global stocks, particularly companies with large international exposure.

2. Earnings take the spotlight: With earnings season picking up, company results are expected to play a bigger role in stock moves than broader macro headlines. Several major companies are set to report, including Netflix (NFLX), Intel (INTC), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), United Airlines (UAL), 3M (MMM), and Charles Schwab (SCHW).

The focus is on company outlooks rather than past results. Investors want to know if Netflix can keep growing subscribers and whether Intel is seeing a pickup in demand. Attention will also be on how Johnson & Johnson's drug business is holding up, and on what United Airlines, 3M, and Charles Schwab say about costs, margins, and client activity.

3. Interest rates remain a key point: Interest rates remain a key factor for markets. Investors are watching the Federal Reserve closely for any hints on the timing of future rate cuts. If rates stay high for longer, growth stocks could remain under pressure. However, a softer message from the Fed could offer some support to the market.

4. AI and chip stocks remain in focus: AI-related stocks continue to draw attention, though expectations remain high. Chipmakers saw a brief boost last week after strong results from Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM). The company reported a 35% jump in fourth-quarter profit, beating estimates on strong demand for AI chips. Net income rose to NT$505.74 billion (about $16 billion), while revenue increased 25.5% from a year earlier to NT$1.046 trillion ($33.73 billion), also topping expectations.

Now, investors want to see if other tech companies can back up the AI story with real revenue growth, not just optimism.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: Why Is Palantir Stock (PLTR) Dropping in Pre-Market Today?
data.description:
Palantir (PLTR) shares opened in the red after the extended weekend, down about 3% in premarket hours. Despite no company-specific news, the decline appears tied to investors continuing to digest President Donald Trump's clash with European leaders over Greenland. Meanwhile, investors are also weighing increased scrutiny of defence contracts, after the UK minister confirmed that military data handled by Palantir remains under the Ministry of Defence's control. Year-to-date, PLTR stock is down roughly 4%.

data.author: Kirti Tak

data.title: Gold Marches Towards $5,000 as Trump Toughens Talk Over Greenland
data.description:
The gold price continued on its march towards $5,000 today as President Donald Trump stepped up his rhetoric over Greenland.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Spot gold rocketed 0.66% to a best-ever $4,726.12 in early trading.

Trump Threats Rattle Investors

Donald Trump's threats to take control of Greenland, currently run by Denmark, either through a financial transaction or potentially by military force, continued to rattle investors.

Speaking to reporters ahead of his trip to the Davos summit in Switzerland this week, Trump, who has threatened European allies with additional tariffs of up to 25%, said: "I don't think they're going to push back too much. Look, we have to have it. As I expressed to everyone, very plainly, Greenland is imperative for National and World Security. There can be no going back — On that, everyone agrees!"

European politicians have been left shocked and bemused by the President's stance, which potentially threatens the future of the NATO alliance and decades, if not centuries in the case of the U.K., of close cooperation.

European leaders are playing tough, threatening retaliatory tariff actions, with France requesting the activation of EU's anti-coercion tool, which could see U.S. goods banned from the single market.

Sow Trade Chaos

Wealth Club chief investment strategist Susannah Streeter said: "President Trump's willingness to break old alliances and sow trade chaos, to further what he believes to be in the US interests, are causing anxiety among investors. With both sides so entrenched, negotiation for now looks highly difficult, and it's likely to keep investor nerves on edge."

Gold is traditionally seen as a safe haven for investors in times of political and economic uncertainty. 2025 saw plenty of that with Trump's initial tariff wave and wars in the Middle East and Ukraine.
data.author: David Craik

data.title: Piper Sandler Puts its Weight Behind 2 'Strong Buy' Cancer Stocks
data.description:
Cancer remains one of the world's deadliest challenges. Second only to heart disease, it claims one in every six lives globally – about 10 million deaths each year – and the numbers are still climbing as lifestyle factors such as smoking, poor diet, and inactivity continue to take their toll.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

For the biopharmaceutical industry, that grim reality has translated into an enormous opportunity. According to Precedence Research, the global market for cancer therapies was valued at $86.2 billion in 2024 and rose to $92.5 billion last year. Looking ahead, it is projected to nearly double to $175.2 billion by 2034, fueled by breakthroughs in novel treatment approaches and a growing push toward personalized medicine.

Against this backdrop, Piper Sandler's 5-star analyst Kelsey Goodwin has picked two cancer-focused biopharma stocks she believes are especially well positioned to benefit from these trends.

Using the TipRanks platform, we took a closer look at her picks – and how they stack up against the broader Wall Street consensus. Let's dive in.

Olema Pharmaceuticals (OLMA)

Kicking off Piper Sandler's list is Olema, a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company focused squarely on women's cancers. The company's efforts center on a single, potentially best-in-class breast cancer therapy, palazestrant, which anchors its entire R&D pipeline.

Olema's pipeline currently has 7 tracks based on palazestrant. The research tracks range from Phase 1 to Phase 3, and are testing palazestrant as a monotherapy and as a combo therapy with various other cancer drugs. Several of the clinical studies are being conducted in conjunction with larger partner companies, Novartis and Pfizer.

Palazestrant, also called pala, is a novel compound, designed to be orally dosed. The drug is an ER (estrogen receptor) antagonist, and blocks cancer cell growth and proliferation signals that are ER-mediated. The drug is under investigation as a treatment for ER+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer.

We should note that OLMA stock jumped sharply in November of last year. That jump in value occurred after Roche, a competitor company, announced positive clinical trial results from its own candidate, giredestrant. This is a new drug in the same class as pala, and its success is considered a positive indicator for the future success of palazestrant.

In her comments on Olema, and its leading candidate, Piper Sandler's Goodwin writes: "OLMA is currently advancing a potential best-in-class CERAN/SERD, palazestrant, capable of complete ER antagonism, potent ER degradation, and holds the promise of becoming the next backbone endocrine therapy in the large ER+/HER2- breast cancer market. Following years of the Street equating all SERD/SERM/CERANs, the lead CERANs are pulling ahead on positive competitor data that surprised to the upside in 4Q25 and has begun to revive the bull case for lead oral SERDs. Multiple Ph 3 trials (from both OLMA and competitors) in 2026 should drive additional value for OLMA shares as POS (probability of success) continues to increase for pala ahead of its 1st potential launch next year."

All told, Goodwin rates OLMA stock as Overweight (i.e., Buy), and she gives the stock a $40 price target that implies a 47% upside potential for the coming year. (To watch Goodwin's track record, click here)

The 11 recent analyst reviews here are unanimously positive, giving the stock its Strong Buy consensus rating. The shares are priced at $27.14, and their $42 average price target indicates room for a 55% one-year gain. (See OLMA stock forecast)
data.author: Michael Marcus

data.title: Trump's 200% Tariff Threat on French Wines, Champagnes Drags Down Stocks
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump's threat to impose a 200% tariff on French wines and champagnes dragged down several European stocks on Tuesday. Trump made this move in an effort to push French President Emmanuel Macron into joining his Board of Peace initiative aimed at resolving global conflicts. The Paris-listed shares of luxury giant LVMH (FR:MC), which owns Moët & Chandon, Dom Pérignon, and Veuve Clicquot brands, fell 4.3%. Meanwhile, shares of Remy Cointreau (FR:RCO) and Pernod Ricard (FR:RI) declined 4.4% and 2.1%, respectively.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

European leaders have condemned Trump's fresh tariff threats, calling them "unacceptable." They are reportedly considering countermeasures, with France urging the European Union to use its economic counter-threat, called the "Anti-Coercion Instrument."

Trump's Tariff Threats Impact Global Markets

Major European indexes were under pressure today, as Trump warned on Sunday that he will announce higher tariffs on eight European allies (Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the U.K., the Netherlands, and Finland), starting at 10% on February 1 and increasing to 25% on June 1, if an agreement is not reached to allow the U.S. to "buy" Greenland.

Furthermore, on Tuesday, Trump warned about a 200% tariff on French wine and champagne following reports that France's President Emmanuel Macron was unwilling to join his Board of Peace initiative on Gaza.

"I'll put a 200% tariff on his wines and champagnes, and he'll join, but he doesn't have to join," said Trump when asked about Micron's stance, Reuters reported.

It is worth noting that wines and spirits exported by the European Union to the U.S. currently face a 15% tariff. The U.S. is the largest market for French wine and spirits, with shipments to the country standing at 3.8 billion euros in 2024.

Analyst Ratings on Key French Alcoholic Beverage Stocks

Currently, the consensus rating for both LVMH and Pernod Ricard stocks is a Moderate Buy, while Remy Cointreau has been assigned a Hold consensus rating. According to TipRanks' Stock Comparison Tool, the average price targets for these stocks indicate the highest upside potential in Pernod Ricard.
data.author: Sirisha Bhogaraju

data.title: Stock Market News Today, 1/20/26 – U.S. Stock Futures Plunge amid Trump's Greenland Tariff Threat
data.description:
U.S. stock futures dropped early Tuesday, starting the holiday-shortened trading week on a negative note. President Donald Trump has threatened a new round of tariffs over his demand to acquire Greenland, rattling global markets. U.S. markets were closed on Monday in honor of the Martin Luther King Jr. holiday.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

Futures on the Nasdaq 100 (NDX), the S&P 500 (SPX), and the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) were down 1.76%, 1.51%, and 1.38%, respectively, at 3:51 a.m. EST on January 20.

Last week, all three major averages closed lower, despite the rally in semiconductor stocks on Thursday after Taiwan Semiconductor's (TSM) blockbuster results. The S&P 500 declined 0.4%, the DJIA fell 0.3%, and the Nasdaq Composite dropped 0.7%.

In a Saturday post on Truth Social, Trump announced a 10% U.S. tariff on eight European allies, including the UK, effective February 1. The tariffs will eventually rise to 25% on June 1 and will stay in place until the U.S. strikes a deal to acquire Greenland.

Looking forward, investors will focus on Trump's speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos on Wednesday, where he will discuss the Greenland issue.

The earnings season also remains in focus. Notable companies reporting this week include Netflix (NFLX), United Airlines (UAL), 3M (MMM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), Charles Schwab (SCHW), Intel (INTC), and Visa (V).

Notably, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up, floating near 4.28%. WTI crude oil futures were trending lower, hovering near $59.15 per barrel as of the last check. Additionally, the Gold Spot U.S. dollar price decreased to nearly $4,721 per ounce on Tuesday.

Elsewhere, European indexes opened lower today amid Trump's renewed tariff threats. European countries are reportedly discussing counter-tariffs and wider economic penalties in response.

Moreover, this morning, Trump threatened 200% tariffs on French wine and champagne after reports stated that France's President Macron refused to join his "Board of Peace" on Gaza.

Asia-Pacific Markets Fall

Asia-Pacific markets fell on January 20 due to ongoing geopolitical tensions, including those involving Iran and Greenland.

Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index fell 0.29%. In China, the Shanghai Composite slipped 0.01%, while the Shenzhen Component lost 0.52%. Meanwhile, Japan's Nikkei dropped 1.11%, and the Topix declined 0.84%.

Stay ahead of macro events with our up-to-the-minute Economic Calendar — filter by impact, country, and more.
data.author: Sheryl Sheth

data.title: 'AI War Heats Up': Alibaba Stock (BABA) Slips as ByteDance Ramps Up AI Cloud Push
data.description:
Alibaba Group (BABA) shares were trending lower in pre-market trading as investors reacted to signs of rising competition in China's fast-growing AI cloud market. The pressure comes after ByteDance, the owner of TikTok, stepped up efforts to expand its AI-led cloud business.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

ByteDance has been growing its cloud unit, Volcano Engine, and is using its AI tools to win corporate customers. The company is also offering lower prices to attract new clients. This strategy has made ByteDance more competitive in AI-heavy workloads, where demand from businesses is rising quickly.

These moves are starting to challenge Alibaba's lead in the fast-growing AI cloud space.

Market Share Data Shows ByteDance Closing the Gap

That push is now starting to show up in market share numbers. According to research firm IDC, Alibaba remained China's largest AI cloud provider in the first half of 2025, with about 23% of the market. ByteDance ranked second with close to 13%, making it the fastest-rising challenger in the segment.

While ByteDance's share of China's overall cloud market is still relatively small, analysts believe its sharp focus on AI services is helping it grow much faster than many rivals.

What It Means for Alibaba Investors

Alibaba remains the clear leader in China's AI cloud market, but ByteDance's rapid rise shows that competition is heating up.

As AI demand continues to grow, Alibaba investors will be watching closely to see how well the company defends its position and whether rising rivalry begins to weigh on margins and growth.

Is Alibaba Stock a Buy Now?  

Wall Street remains constructive on the stock. Alibaba stock carries a Strong Buy consensus rating, based on 13 Buy ratings and one Hold over the past three months. The average BABA price target stands at $203.66, suggesting roughly 23.13% upside from current levels.
data.author: Shalu Saraf

data.title: After U.S. Approval, Nvidia's (NVDA) H200 AI Chip Waits on China's Answer
data.description:
NVIDIA (NVDA) received approval from the U.S. last week to sell its H200 AI chip to China under set terms. However, the process has now slowed because China has not cleared the chip for entry. According to reports, Chinese customs have told agents that the H200 is not allowed into the country for now. As a result, shipments remain on hold even though the U.S. side has agreed.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

This creates an unusual setup for investors to track. In most past cases, U.S. export rules were the main limit on chip sales to China. In this case, the delay appears to be coming from Beijing instead.

On Friday, NVDA shares dropped 0.44% to close at $186.23.
data.author: Ran Melamed

data.title: NVDA, GOOGL, AMZN: Trump's Greenland Gambit Rattles Big Tech as EU Weighs Salvo
data.description:
President Donald Trump's tariff threat tied to his Greenland acquisition ambitions, along with the EU's mooted $108 billion tariff package and "trade bazooka," sank tech shares in U.S. pre-market trading.

Claim 50% Off TipRanks Premium

  • Unlock hedge fund-level data and powerful investing tools for smarter, sharper decisions
  • Stay ahead of the market with the latest news and analysis and maximize your portfolio's potential

As trading resumed on Tuesday following Monday's federal holidays, Big Tech stocks fell, mimicking the fall in U.S. stock futures and a similar drop in Europe seen earlier on Monday. As of 4:42 a.m. EST:

  • Nvidia (NVDA) — dropped 3.17% to $180.27
  • Alphabet (GOOGL) — tumbled 4% to $316.86
  • Amazon (AMZN) — shrank 3.20% to $218.48
  • Microsoft (MSFT) — edged lower 1.71% to $451.98
  • Meta (META) — fell about 3% to $601.71

Over the weekend, President Trump warned of an additional 10% tariff on eight European countries, starting February 1, with plans to raise them to 25% by June 1 until a deal is reached on his proposal to make Greenland part of the U.S. In addition to the EU countries of Denmark, Sweden, France, Germany and the Netherland, the threat covers non-members, the UK and Norway.

What EU and U.S. Stocks Could Be in Crosshairs?

Trump's new tariffs could leave several European companies, including Danish drugmaker Novo Nordisk (NVO), Switzerland-based Roche (RHHBY), and France's Sanofi (SNY), exposed to additional export burden. In response, the EU is considering letting its suspended retaliatory tariff package on $108 billion American goods come into effect in February.

Such a move could hit several U.S. companies such as Tyson Foods (TSN), Harley-Davidson (HOG) and Caterpillar (CAT).

In addition, the EU is weighing invoking its anti-coercion instrument, colloquially known as the "trade bazooka." However, EU leaders will meet this Thursday in Brussels for an emergency summit to discuss their options.

data.author: Solomon Oladipupo

data.title: NFLX vs. DIS: Which Stock Is the Better Buy Before Earnings?
data.description:
Streaming giants Netflix (NFLX) and Disney (DIS) are set to report their quarterly earnings, and investors are weighing which stock offers the better opportunity. Netflix will release Q4 2025 results on January 20, while Disney is scheduled to report Q1 FY26 on February 2. Ahead of earnings, Disney stock carries a Strong Buy rating from analysts, with upside potential of over 20%. In comparison, Netflix has a Moderate Buy rating but offers a higher potential upside of more than 40%.
